ORDER STAYING ACTION

LAW AND MOTION 302, THE COURT CONFIRMS THE TENTATIVE RULING. DEFENDANT SWISSPORT SA, LLC, SWISSPORT USA, INC. NOTICE OF MOTION AND MOTION TO COMPEL ARBITRATION. THE CASE IS STAYED IN ITS ENTIRETY PENDING THE CALIFORNIA SUPREME COURT'S FORTHCOMING DECISION IN ZB, N.A. V. SUPERIOR COURT (LAWSON), CASE NO. S246711. (SEE MINI-MINUTES FOR DETAILS) THE COURT FINDS GOOD CAUSE TO AWAIT GUIDANCE FROM THE SUPREME COURT BEFORE DECIDING THIS MOTION. ORAL ARGUMENT WAS HELD ON JUNE 5, 2019, AND THE COURT HAS REQUESTED SUPPLEMENTAL BRIEFS BE FILED ON JUNE 20 AND JUNE 27, 2019, AT WHICH TIME THE MATTER WILL BE RESUBMITTED. A FINAL DECISION SHOULD FOLLOW WITHIN 90 DAYS. AFTER THE SUPREME COURT ISSUES ITS RULING, THE PARTIES SHOULD CONTACT THIS COURT TO SET A NEW HEARING DATE ON THE MOTION. WRITTEN ORDER IS SIGNED AND FILED. JUDGE: ETHAN P. SCHULMAN; CLERK: J. RIOS MERIDA; REPORTER: ANTHONY VAUGHN, CSR NO. 6185, TONYVAUGHN@YAHOO.COM. =(302/EPS)

LAW AND MOTION 302, DEFENDANTS SWISSPORT SA, LLC, SWISSPORT USA, INC.'S MOTION TO COMPEL ARBITRATION IS CONTINUED TO DECEMBER 19, 2019, TO BE HEARD BY JUDGE SCHULMAN. IN ADDITION, THE PARTIES ARE ORDERED TO PROVIDE COURTESY COPIES OF THEIR FILINGS PER SF LOCAL RULE 2.7B WITH A COVER LETTER REFLECTING THE NEW HEARING DATE. JUDGE: HAROLD E. KAHN; CLERK: GINA GONZALES; NOT REPORTED.

Notice Of Motion And Motion To Stay; Memorandum Of Points And Authorities In Support Thereof
Off Calendar Feb-14-2020 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:

Appearances:
Sarah Hamilton, Esq. and Cody T. Stroman, Esq. of CONSTANGY BROOKS SITH & PROPHETE LLP, T:415.918.3020, for defendant/moving party;
Julia Z. Wells, Esq. of MATERN LAW GROUP PC, T:310.531.1900, for plaintiff/opposing party, on Court Call.

RULING: The tentative ruling was contested and argued. After arguments, the Court adopted its tentative ruling, as follows:

Defendants Swissport SA, LLC and Swissport USA, Inc.'s motion to stay is granted based on the doctrine of exclusive concurrent jurisdiction. The exclusive concurrent jurisdiction rule provides: "where two (or more) courts possess concurrent subject matter jurisdiction over a cause, the court that first asserts jurisdiction assumes it to the exclusion of all others, thus rendering 'concurrent' jurisdiction 'exclusive' with the first court." (Franklin & Franklin v. 7-Eleven Owners for Fair Franchising (2000) 85 Cal.App.4th 1168, 1175.) The rule of exclusive concurrent jurisdiction "is meant to avoid the spectacle of the same parties litigating the same issues in two different courts at the same time, including the real possibility of 'unseemly conflict between courts that might arise if they were free to make contradictory decisions or awards at the same time or relating to the same controversy; another reason is to protect litigants from the expense and harassment of multiple litigation." (Id. at 1176.) "That the parties in the two actions 'are not entirely identical' and that 'the remedies sought by the two actions are not precisely the same' is not controlling." (Id.) Under the rule, "when two courts have concurrent jurisdiction over the subject matter and the parties, the first court to assume jurisdiction has exclusive and continuing jurisdiction 'until such time as all necessarily related matters have been resolved.'" (County of Siskiyou v. Superior Court (2013) 217 Cal.App.4th 83, 91.) While PAGA itself may not preclude separate but similar actions by different employees against the same employer, (Julian v. Glenair, Inc. (2017) 17 Cal.App.5th 853, 866), that does not prevent the Court from staying this action under this rule. Whether or not such a stay is mandatory, plaintiffs concede that at a minimum, the Court retains discretion to stay this case in light of overlapping litigation in other courts.

Here, as alleged, plaintiff's instant claims are entirely subsumed within John Jackson v. Swissport SA, LLC, Case No. BC694782 ("J. Jackson action.") (J. Jackson Amended Complaint, Para. 11; Plaintiff's Complaint, Para. 10.) The J. Jackson action was filed first, apparently is further along in discovery, and is set for trial first, and its amended complaint seeks recovery on behalf of "all other aggrieved employees of Defendants." (J. Jackson Amended Complaint, Para. 1.) In other words, the J. Jackson case is presently not limited to LAX workers, but covers all of defendants' workers.

Order signed in open court.

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman; Clerk: Gina Gonzales; Court Reporter: Maria A. Torreano CSR#8600, email: maria.torreano@gmail.com

CASE MANAGEMENT CONFERENCE
Off Calendar Dec-26-2019 Set For Trial On Jan-04-21 At 09:30 A.M. In Dept 206

Notice Of Motion To Compel Arbitration
Off Calendar Dec-19-2019 Denied
MINI MINUTES: No appearances. The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

DEFENDANT SWISSPORT SA, LLC, SWISSPORT USA, INC.'S Motion To Compel Arbitration.

Defendants' Swissport SA, LLC and Swissport USA, Inc.'s motion to compel arbitration is denied.

The complaint in this action solely asserts representative claims under the California Private Attorneys General Act of 2004 (PAGA) for civil penalties for Defendants' alleged violations of the California Labor Code governing, among other things, provision of meal and rest breaks, payment of wages, provision of itemized wage statements, and indemnification of expenditures. Claims under PAGA are not arbitrable. (Iskanian v. CLS Transportation Los Angeles, LLC (2014) 59 Cal.4th 348, 384 ["where, as here, an employment agreement compels the waiver of representative claims under PAGA, it is contrary to public policy and unenforceable as a matter of state law."]; see also, e.g., ZA, N.A. v. Superior Court (2019) 8 Cal.5th 175, 197 ["Iskanian established an important principle: employers cannot compel employees to waive their right to enforce the state's interests when the PAGA has empowered employees to do so."].)

This court previously stayed this action pending the California Supreme Court's decision in Z.B., N.A. In that case, the Court held that employees cannot recover unpaid wages described in Labor Code section 558 in a PAGA claim. (8 Cal.5th at 198 ["a PAGA claim does not include unpaid wages under section 558."].) Accordingly, the Court affirmed the Court of Appeal's judgment ordering the trial court to deny the employer's motion to compel arbitration. (Id.) Here, Plaintiff represents that in view of that holding, she "no longer intends to seek unpaid wages pursuant to Labor Code section 558," and that she is willing to stipulate to amend the complaint to strike any language which purports to seek such recovery. Accordingly, the motion to compel arbitration must be denied.

Order is signed. (see scanned order for details)

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman, Clerk: M. Goodman, Court Reporter: Maria Torreano, CSR#8600, maria.torreano@gmail.com, Not Reported. =(302/EPS)

Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Arbitration
Off Calendar Jun-24-2019 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
No appearances.

The Court confirms the tentative ruling.

DEFENDANT SWISSPORT SA, LLC, SWISSPORT USA, INC. Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Arbitration

The case is stayed in its entirety pending the California Supreme Court's forthcoming decision in ZB, N.A. v. Superior Court (Lawson), Case No. S246711. (See Freiberg v. City of Mission Viejo (1995) 33 Cal.App.4th 1484, 1489 ["Trial courts generally have the inherent power to stay proceedings in the interests of justice and to promote judicial efficiency."].)
The threshold issues presented by this motion to compel arbitration are (1) how unpaid wages recoverable under Labor Code section 558 brought as part of a PAGA action are properly allocated between the plaintiff and the State Labor and Workforce Development Agency (LWDA) and (2) how that allocation impacts the arbitrability of section 558 claims brought as part of a PAGA action. California's appellate courts are split on this question. (Compare Zakaryan v. The Men's Wearhouse, Inc. (2019) 33 Cal.App.5th 659, 674-676 [section 558 claim not arbitrable as part of PAGA action, but 75% of the recovery of unpaid wages must be paid to LWDA]; Lawson v. ZB, N.A. (2017) 18 Cal.App.5th 705, 721, 724-725, review granted [section 558 claims not arbitrable and plaintiff is entitled to 100% of unpaid wages recovered]; and Esparza v. KS Industries, L.P. (2017) 13 Cal.App.5th 1228, 1246, review denied [section 558 claim is a private claim for unpaid wages that should be split from the PAGA action and arbitrated].) The Court finds good cause to await guidance from the Supreme Court before deciding this motion. Oral argument was held on June 5, 2019, and the Court has requested supplemental briefs be filed on June 20 and June 27, 2019, at which time the matter will be resubmitted. A final decision should follow within 90 days. After the Supreme Court issues its ruling, the parties should contact this Court to set a new hearing date on the motion.

Written order is signed and filed.

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman; Clerk: J. Rios Merida; Reporter: Anthony Vaughn, CSR No. 6185, tonyvaughn@yahoo.com
